findings.title: Token expiry and single-use redemption are implemented and tested
findings.claim: AC1 is satisfied: the injected-clock/token-generator store rejects tokens at or after expiry and permits only one successful redemption.
findings.rationale: The committed implementation stores an expiry and state for each token, rejects when the clock is at or beyond expiry, and transitions successfully redeemed tokens to consumed. Tests cover the exact expiry boundary, default lifetime, repeated redemption, invalid lifetimes, and invalid generated tokens.
findings.remediation: None for the declared single-process demonstration scope.

findings.title: Neutral responses and equal deferred scheduling are implemented and tested
findings.claim: AC2 is satisfied: known and unknown accounts receive the same response, and each request enqueues exactly one account-dependent job before returning.
findings.rationale: The handler always invokes the injected scheduler once with lookup, issuance, and delivery inside the deferred job. Deterministic tests establish response equality, equal scheduling, and absence of lookup or delivery on the response path.
findings.remediation: None for the declared demonstration scope.

findings.title: Telemetry is identifier-free and audit invocation is safe
findings.claim: AC3 and AC4 are satisfied: raw tokens and direct account identifiers are excluded from logs and audit details, while a fixed safe audit event is invoked.
findings.rationale: The implementation emits only a constant log message and a constant audit event/detail. Tests compare complete telemetry for known and unknown accounts and assert that synthetic email, account, token, and long hexadecimal identifier patterns are absent.
findings.remediation: None. Audit delivery remains intentionally best-effort so a stalled transport cannot delay the neutral response.

findings.title: Concurrent redemption and retry semantics are deterministic
findings.claim: AC5 and AC6 are satisfied within the declared single-process atomicity boundary.
findings.rationale: The token state changes synchronously from active to redeeming before the reset action is awaited, causing a concurrent attempt to fail. Consumption occurs only after successful completion; rejection restores active state so a still-valid token may be retried. The callback idempotency or rollback requirement is explicitly documented and tested for rejection followed by retry.
findings.remediation: None for this demonstration. A production multi-worker implementation would require shared persistent atomic storage.

findings.title: Dependency failures retain the neutral response
findings.claim: AC7 is satisfied for scheduling, lookup, issuance, delivery, logging, and audit failures.
findings.rationale: Each dependency boundary is isolated from the public response. Account-dependent exceptions are contained in the worker, scheduler and logging exceptions are caught, and audit rejection or synchronous failure is contained. Tests cover every listed failure and additionally verify that a stalled audit transport does not delay the response.
findings.remediation: None for the stated criteria.

findings.title: Required deterministic automated coverage passes post-commit
findings.claim: AC8 is satisfied by 16 passing tests bound to final commit a5d1093d7e1b65bd8d874b16ce832ba0840d6452.
findings.rationale: The supplied final artifacts contain tests for known and unknown accounts, equal scheduling, deferred lookup and delivery, response equality, safe telemetry, lifetime validation, generated-token validation, unknown tokens, exact expiry, repeated and concurrent redemption, retry after failure, all listed dependency failures, and stalled audit behavior. The post-commit npm test result passed with exit code 0.
findings.remediation: None.

findings.title: Final evidence handoff is integrity-bound and scoped clean
findings.claim: The supplied final state binds the source, tests, task, and package manifest to a specific commit and passing post-commit test.
findings.rationale: The final-git-state record identifies commit a5d1093d7e1b65bd8d874b16ce832ba0840d6452, reports the scoped paths clean, includes artifact content and SHA-256 digests, and references the passing post-commit test. Earlier working-tree modifications are superseded by this final committed state.
findings.remediation: None.

limitations: Assessment is limited to the supplied evidence digest; no commands were independently executed and no files outside the embedded final artifacts were inspected.

The in-memory token store is atomic only within one JavaScript process and does not survive restart. Multi-process or production use requires a persistent shared store with transactional or compare-and-set semantics.
The injected scheduler is a deterministic demonstration boundary, not a durable production queue with retries, monitoring, or delivery guarantees.
Audit submission is best-effort and non-blocking; the implementation guarantees safe invocation and neutral response behavior, not durable audit persistence.
Repository-wide canonical validation, hosted verification, deployment proof, release checks, and human acceptance were explicitly outside the supplied task scope and remain unavailable.

findings.title: Password-reset enumeration controls are implemented and tested
findings.claim: Known and unknown accounts receive the same neutral response, while account lookup, token issuance, and delivery are deferred behind one scheduler job per request. Telemetry excludes email addresses, account identifiers, tokens, and dependency failure details.
findings.rationale: The committed implementation schedules account-dependent work before returning a constant response and uses fixed log and audit payloads. Tests cover response equality, equal scheduling, deferred lookup and delivery, safe telemetry, and dependency failures. The post-commit suite passed.
findings.remediation: None within the demonstration scope.

findings.title: Token expiry, single use, and deterministic concurrent reservation are implemented
findings.claim: The in-memory token store rejects tokens at or after expiry, reserves a token synchronously before awaiting reset work, permits only one concurrent redemption, and consumes the token only after successful reset action completion.
findings.rationale: The implementation contains explicit active, redeeming, and consumed states with an injected clock and generator. Deterministic tests cover the expiry boundary, repeated redemption, concurrent redemption, failed-action retry, invalid lifetimes, duplicate and empty tokens, and unknown tokens.
findings.remediation: None within the single-process demonstration scope.

findings.title: Stalled audit transport no longer delays the neutral response
findings.claim: Audit dispatch is best-effort and is not awaited on the response path; synchronous throws and asynchronous rejections are isolated.
findings.rationale: The final source invokes audit with a fixed identifier-free event and attaches rejection handling without awaiting completion. A regression test verifies that a pending audit promise does not block the response, and the committed test suite passed.
findings.remediation: None for this demonstration. A production system should deliver audit events through a reliable, bounded, privacy-preserving mechanism.

findings.title: Final evidence is bound to a clean committed state
findings.claim: The supplied final artifacts are associated with commit a5d1093d7e1b65bd8d874b16ce832ba0840d6452, have recorded SHA-256 digests, are scoped clean, and passed the post-commit test command.
findings.rationale: The final-git-state record binds the source, tests, task, and package manifest to the commit and references a passing post-commit test. The source, test, and task hashes also agree with the pre-commit validation output.
findings.remediation: None for the supplied evidence-integrity claim.

limitations: The token store is intentionally in-memory and its reservation transition is atomic only within one JavaScript process. It does not survive restarts or coordinate multiple workers; production use requires a persistent shared store with transactional or compare-and-set semantics.

Token entropy and unpredictability depend entirely on the injected generateToken implementation. The demonstration uses synthetic deterministic values and does not establish production cryptographic token generation.
The scheduler contract is injected and demonstrated with an in-memory deferred scheduler. Durability, bounded retries, monitoring, and delivery guarantees are outside the supplied scope.
Successful audit persistence is not guaranteed because dispatch is best-effort; the evidence establishes safe invocation and response isolation, not durable audit receipt.

Rate limiting, abuse prevention, user-session invalidation, authorization around the final password change, transport security, data retention, dependency provenance, hosted behavior, and deployment controls were not demonstrated and are outside the stated acceptance criteria.
Only the supplied artifacts and recorded tests were reviewed; repository-wide canonical validation, hosted verification, release checks, and human acceptance remain unavailable.

findings.title: Acceptance criteria have broad deterministic automated coverage
findings.claim: The committed suite covers the required positive, negative, boundary, concurrency, failure-isolation, and regression scenarios within the synthetic workspace.
findings.rationale: The 16 tests cover known and unknown accounts, equal scheduling, deferred lookup and delivery, neutral response equality, identifier-free telemetry, exact expiry, repeated and concurrent redemption, retry after callback failure, invalid lifetimes, invalid generated tokens, unknown tokens, and failures across scheduler, lookup, issuance, delivery, logging, and audit dependencies. The post-commit test run passed at the artifact-bound commit.
findings.remediation: None required for the declared demonstration scope.

findings.title: Stalled-audit response-path regression is covered
findings.claim: A deterministic regression test verifies that an audit promise which remains unsettled does not delay the neutral public response.
findings.rationale: The final implementation invokes audit best-effort without awaiting transport completion, while handling synchronous throws and promise rejection. The added test holds the audit promise open, awaits the request result, and verifies the neutral response before releasing the audit.
findings.remediation: None required.

findings.title: Evidence integrity is bound to a clean committed state
findings.claim: The supplied final artifacts, hashes, scoped Git state, commit, and post-commit passing test are coherently linked.
findings.rationale: The final-git-state record reports scopedClean=true at commit a5d1093d7e1b65bd8d874b16ce832ba0840d6452, includes the artifact contents and SHA-256 digests, and references a successful post-commit npm test run. The source and test hashes match the earlier validation output.
findings.remediation: None required.

limitations: The evidence demonstrates only the supplied single-process synthetic workspace; no hosted, browser, deployment, or repository-wide validation was supplied or required.

The in-memory token store does not test cross-process atomicity, persistence across restart, or integration with a durable queue/shared transactional store. These are explicitly outside the demonstration boundary.
Audit tests establish safe invocation and response-path isolation, not guaranteed durable audit persistence when the transport stalls or fails.

No external email provider, datastore, queue, or end-to-end HTTP integration tests are present; dependency-injected unit tests are adequate for the declared narrow scope but do not establish production integration behavior.

findings.title: Acceptance criteria are implemented and covered by the committed test suite
findings.claim: The scoped password-reset demonstration implements expiring single-use tokens, neutral responses, deferred account-dependent work, identifier-free telemetry, deterministic single-process redemption, retry after failed reset action, and dependency-failure isolation.
findings.rationale: The final bound source implements the required token state transitions and scheduler boundary. The final test artifact contains deterministic coverage corresponding to all eight acceptance criteria, and the linked post-commit test completed successfully at the final commit.
findings.remediation: None for the declared demonstration scope.

findings.title: Final-state evidence has commit, artifact, hash, cleanliness, and post-commit test binding
findings.claim: The supplied evidence establishes a clean scoped final state at commit a5d1093d7e1b65bd8d874b16ce832ba0840d6452 and binds the covered artifacts to a passing post-commit test.
findings.rationale: The final-git-state record names the final commit, reports scopedClean=true, embeds the four scoped artifacts with SHA-256 digests, and references the passing post-commit test. The source, test, and task-file hashes also match the earlier validation output, supporting continuity between validation and the committed state.
findings.remediation: None.

findings.title: Audit delivery is intentionally best-effort rather than durably guaranteed
findings.claim: The handler initiates a safe audit event without awaiting transport completion, so a stalled or failed audit sink cannot delay the neutral response; this does not prove durable audit recording.
findings.rationale: The final implementation calls audit with fixed identifier-free data and suppresses synchronous and asynchronous failures. The regression test proves a stalled audit does not block the response. This satisfies response isolation and demonstrates safe audit invocation, but durable persistence is outside the injected interface and evidence.
findings.remediation: If durable recording is required beyond this demonstration, enqueue the audit event to a durable outbox or queue while preserving the neutral response path.

findings.title: Atomicity and scheduling guarantees are scoped to one process and injected test boundaries
findings.claim: The demonstration does not establish multi-process token atomicity, restart survival, or durable background scheduling.
findings.rationale: The implementation and task contract explicitly limit the token store to one JavaScript process and identify the production need for a persistent compare-and-set or transaction. The scheduler contract likewise recommends a durable production queue. These are declared out-of-scope production limitations and do not contradict the synthetic acceptance criteria.
findings.remediation: For production use, replace the in-memory store with shared persistent transactional storage and use a durable monitored queue with bounded retries.

limitations: The post-commit test evidence reports success and exit code 0 but does not include individual test output or an exact post-commit pass count; the earlier pre-commit validation reports 16 passed and 0 failed.
Repository-wide canonical validation, hosted verification, deployment proof, release checks, and human acceptance were explicitly unavailable and are outside the supplied task scope.
No independent execution was performed during this review; conclusions rely solely on the supplied evidence digest and its final-state binding.
The evidence proves deterministic behavior for the synthetic test environment, not real-world timing indistinguishability under production schedulers, load, or network conditions.

output.summary: All four specialist reviews conclude that the scoped password-reset demonstration satisfies AC1–AC8 and that its final evidence is coherently bound to commit a5d1093d7e1b65bd8d874b16ce832ba0840d6452 with a clean scoped state and passing post-commit test. The implementation demonstrates expiring single-use tokens, neutral and deferred request handling, identifier-free telemetry, deterministic single-process concurrency and retry behavior, and dependency-failure isolation. Readiness applies only to the declared synthetic, single-process scope; durable audit delivery, persistent cross-process atomicity, production scheduling, and deployment behavior were not established.

findings.title: Acceptance criteria are satisfied within the declared demonstration scope
findings.claim: The supplied evidence supports AC1–AC8 for the scoped single-process password-reset demonstration.
findings.rationale: The specialist reviews consistently report deterministic coverage of token expiry and single use, neutral responses, equal deferred scheduling, safe telemetry and audit invocation, concurrent redemption, retry after reset failure, dependency-failure isolation, and positive, negative, boundary, and regression cases. The artifact-bound post-commit test reports success.
findings.remediation: None for the declared demonstration scope.

findings.title: Final evidence is bound to a clean committed state
findings.claim: The final source, tests, task, and package manifest are reported as bound by hashes and scoped cleanliness to commit a5d1093d7e1b65bd8d874b16ce832ba0840d6452 and a passing post-commit test.
findings.rationale: The final-state record identifies the commit, reports scopedClean=true, embeds artifact digests, and references the successful post-commit test. The reviews also report continuity with earlier validation hashes.
findings.remediation: None for the narrow supplied evidence-integrity claim.

findings.title: Audit invocation is response-safe but not durably persisted
findings.claim: Audit dispatch uses fixed identifier-free data and does not block the neutral response, but the evidence does not establish durable audit receipt.
findings.rationale: The implementation reportedly contains synchronous throws and asynchronous rejections and includes a regression test showing that a pending audit promise does not delay the response. Best-effort invocation satisfies the stated scope but is not a durable recording guarantee.
findings.remediation: If durable audit recording becomes a requirement, use a privacy-preserving transactional outbox or durable queue without placing transport completion on the public response path.

findings.title: Atomicity and scheduling guarantees do not extend beyond one process
findings.claim: The demonstration does not establish cross-process token atomicity, restart persistence, or durable background-job execution.
findings.rationale: The supplied artifacts and reviews explicitly scope atomic state transitions to one JavaScript process and use an injected demonstration scheduler. These limitations do not contradict the current acceptance criteria but prevent extrapolation to a multi-worker production system.
findings.remediation: For production use, adopt shared persistent storage with transactional or compare-and-set redemption and a durable monitored queue with bounded retries.

limitations: No commands were independently executed; this synthesis relies solely on the supplied evidence digest.
The post-commit test record reports success and exit code 0 but does not include individual test output or an exact pass count; the earlier validation reports 16 passing tests.
The evidence covers a synthetic dependency-injected workspace, not hosted behavior, end-to-end HTTP integration, external email, datastore, or queue integrations.

Production concerns including cryptographic token generation, rate limiting, abuse prevention, session invalidation, transport security, authorization around password changes, deployment controls, and dependency provenance were outside scope.
Repository-wide canonical validation, release checks, deployment proof, and human acceptance were not supplied.
The evidence does not prove real-world timing indistinguishability under production load, network variance, or scheduler behavior.
This synthesis is model judgement over supplied evidence, not an independent certification or security guarantee.
